name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ٱقْرَأْ بِٱسْمِ رَبِّكَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ (١)
```

```english
Read in the Name of your Lord who created; (1)
```

```arabic
خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ (٢)
```

```english
created man from a clinging mass. (2)
```

```arabic
ٱقْرَأْ وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْأَكْرَمُ (٣)
```

```english
Read, and your Lord is the most generous, (3)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَ بِٱلْقَلَمِ (٤)
```

```english
who taught by the pen, (4)
```

```arabic
عَلَّمَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ مَا لَمْ يَعْلَمْ (٥)
```

```english
taught man what he did not know. (5)
```

```arabic
كَلَّآ إِنَّ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ لَيَطْغَىٰٓ (٦)
```

```english
Indeed man becomes rebellious (6)
```

```arabic
أَن رَّءَاهُ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰٓ (٧)
```

```english
when he considers himself without need. (7)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ٱلرُّجْعَىٰٓ (٨)
```

```english
Indeed to your Lord is the return. (8)
```

```arabic
أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يَنْهَىٰ (٩)
```

```english
Tell me, he who forbids (9)
```

```arabic
عَبْدًا إِذَا صَلَّىٰٓ (١٠)
```

```english
a servant when he prays, (10)
```

```arabic
أَرَءَيْتَ إِن كَانَ عَلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (١١)
```

```english
tell me, should he be on [true] guidance, (11)
```

```arabic
أَوْ أَمَرَ بِٱلتَّقْوَىٰٓ (١٢)
```

```english
or bid [others] to Godwariness, (12)
```

```arabic
أَرَءَيْتَ إِن كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّىٰٓ (١٣)
```

```english
tell me, should he call him a liar and turn away (13)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ يَعْلَم بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرَىٰ (١٤)
```

```english
—does he not know that Allah sees [him]? (14)
```

```arabic
كَلَّا لَئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ لَنَسْفَعًۢا بِٱلنَّاصِيَةِ (١٥)
```

```english
No indeed! If he does not cease, We shall seize him by the forelock, (15)
```

```arabic
نَاصِيَةٍ كَـٰذِبَةٍ خَاطِئَةٍ (١٦)
```

```english
a lying, sinful forelock! (16)
```

```arabic
فَلْيَدْعُ نَادِيَهُۥ (١٧)
```

```english
Then let him call out his gang! (17)
```

```arabic
سَنَدْعُ ٱلزَّبَانِيَةَ (١٨)
```

```english
We [too] shall call the keepers of hell. (18)
```

```arabic
كَلَّا لَا تُطِعْهُ وَٱسْجُدْ وَٱقْتَرِب ۩ (١٩)
```

```english
No indeed! Do not obey him, but prostrate and draw near [to Allah]! (19)
```

## (Commentary)

This is a command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to receive the Quran through revelation (الوحي) from Him, the Exalted. It is the first Surah of the Quran to be revealed. The context of its verses does not preclude its revelation all at once, as we shall point out, and it is definitively Meccan (مكية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ** — **Read in the name of your Lord who created; created man from a clinging mass.** (96:1-2). Al-Raghib said: "Reading (القراءة) is the joining of letters and words to one another in recitation (الترتيل). This is not said for every type of gathering; one does not say, 'I read the people' when gathering them. Evidence for this is that it is not called 'reading' when a single letter is uttered." (End of quote).

In any case, it is said: "I read the book" when one gathers the letters and words contained therein by joining them to one another in the mind, even if they are not articulated. It is also said: "I read it" when one gathers the letters and words by joining them to one another in articulation. Furthermore, it is said: "I read it to him" when one joins its letters and words within his hearing; in this sense, it is also termed "recitation" (التلاوة). The Exalted said: **رَسُولٌ مِنَ اللهِ يَتْلُوا صُحُفاً مُطَهَّرَةً** — **an apostle from Allah reciting purified manuscripts.** (98:2).

The apparent meaning of His absolute command **اقْرَأْ** (Read) is the first sense, and what is intended is the command to receive what the Angel of Revelation (ملك الوحي) reveals to him of the Quran. Thus, the sentence is a command to read the Book, and it is itself part of the Book, similar to a person saying at the opening of a letter to the one he sent it to: "Read this letter of mine and act upon it." This statement of his is a command to read the letter, yet it is part of the letter itself.

This context supports, firstly, what has been narrated that these verses were the very first of the Quran to be revealed to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

Secondly, the implied object is "Read the Quran" or something similar. It does not mean "reading" in an absolute sense by using the verb intransitively while disregarding the object, nor does it mean "reading to the people" by omitting the related object—even if that was among the purposes of the revelation, as He said: **وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً** — **We have sent the Quran in [separate] parts so that you may read it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in successive revelations.** (17:106). Nor is His saying **بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ** (in the name of your Lord) the direct object of "Read" with the preposition *ba* (ب) being redundant—meaning "Read the name of your Lord," i.e., recite the *Basmalah*.

Rather, His saying **بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ** is connected to an implied word such as "opening" or "beginning," or it is connected to "Read" where the preposition *ba* denotes accompaniment (الملابسة). This does not contradict the fact that the *Basmalah* with which the Surah begins is a part of the Surah; it is from the speech of Allah with which He, the Glorified, opened [the Surah] and commanded that it be read starting with it, just as He commanded that His saying **اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ** (Read in the name...) etc., be read. Thus, it contains an instruction through action, similar to the command regarding the exception (الاستثناء) in His saying: **وَلا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللهُ** — **Do not say about anything, ‘I will do that tomorrow,’ without [adding], ‘if Allah wishes.’** (18:23-24). Understand this well.

In His saying **رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ** (your Lord who created), there is an indication of the restriction of Lordship (الربوبية) to Allah, exalted is His Name—which is the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His governance and sustaining of creation* — which necessitates the restriction of worship (العبادة) to Him. For the polytheists used to say: "Allah, the Glorified, possesses only creation and origination, whereas Lordship—which is sovereignty and governance—belongs to the near ones among His creation from the angels, jinn, and humans." So Allah refuted this with His saying **رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ**, which stipulates that both Lordship and creation belong to Him alone.

His saying **خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ** (created man from a clinging mass): what is intended is the human genus that reproduces, and the "clinging mass" (العلق) is congealed blood, referring to what the sperm-drop (النطفة) transforms into within the womb.

The verse contains an indication of the Divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) that oversees man from the moment he was a clinging mass until he becomes a complete and perfect human being, possessing such wondrous attributes and actions that intellects are bewildered by them. Man did not become a human being nor reach perfection except through successive governance from Him, the Exalted, which is itself "creation after creation." Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord who governs the affairs of man by the very fact that He is his Creator. Therefore, man has no choice but to take Him alone as Lord. Thus, the discourse contains an argument for the monotheism of Lordship.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اقْرَأْ وَرَبُّكَ الْأَكْرَمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ** — **Read, and your Lord is the most generous, who taught by the pen, taught man what he did not know.** (96:3-5). This is a second command to read, as an emphasis of the first command, according to the apparent absolute context.

It has been said: What is intended is the command to read to the people, which is the proclamation (التبليغ), unlike the first command, which was intended as a command to read for himself. It has also been said: Both commands together mean the command to read to the people. Both views are not apparent.

His saying **وَرَبُّكَ الْأَكْرَمُ** (and your Lord is the Most Generous) means He whose giving surpasses the giving of all others. He, the Exalted, gives not out of entitlement, and there is no blessing except that its bestowal traces back to Him, the Exalted.

His saying **الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ** (who taught by the pen): the preposition *ba* denotes causality (السببية), meaning He taught reading or writing and reading by means of the pen. The sentence is either a circumstantial clause (حالية) or an initial one (استئنافية). The discourse is aimed at strengthening the soul of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and removing anxiety and agitation from it, as he was commanded to read while being unlettered (أمي), neither writing nor reading. It is as if it were said: "Read the Book of your Lord which He reveals to you and do not fear, for your Lord is the Most Generous who taught man reading by means of the pen with which he writes; thus He is capable of teaching you the reading of His Book while you are unlettered. He has commanded you to read, and had He not empowered you to do so, He would not have commanded you."

Then He, the Glorified, generalized the blessing, mentioning His teaching man what he did not know, saying: **عَلَّمَ الْإِنْسانَ ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ** (taught man what he did not know). In this is a further strengthening of the heart of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a comforting of his soul.

What is intended by "man" is the genus, as is apparent from the context. It has been said: It refers to Adam (عليه‌السلام). It has also been said: It refers to Idris (عليه‌السلام) because he was the first to write with the pen. It has also been said: Every prophet who used to write. These are weak views far from [correct] understanding.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى** — **Indeed! Man becomes rebellious when he deems himself self-sufficient.** (96:6-7). This is a rebuff (ردع) of what might be inferred from the previous verses—that He, the Exalted, bestowed upon man great blessings such as teaching by the pen, other things He taught, and teaching through the way of revelation (الوحي). Thus, it is incumbent upon man to thank Him for that, yet he disbelieves in His blessing and rebels.

His saying **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَيَطْغى** (Indeed, man becomes rebellious) means he exceeds his bounds. This is an informative statement about what is in the nature of man, similar to His saying: **إِنَّ الْإِنْسانَ لَظَلُومٌ كَفَّارٌ** — **Indeed man is most unfair and ungrateful.** (14:34).

His saying **أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى** (when he deems himself self-sufficient): the verb "saw" (رأى) here denotes opinion/belief (الرأي) rather than visual sight. The subject of "saw" and its object is "man." The clause **أَنْ رَآهُ اسْتَغْنى** serves as a causal explanation, meaning: he rebels because he believes himself to be independent of his Lord who blessed him, and thus he disbelieves in Him. This is because he becomes occupied with himself and the apparent causes through which he reaches his goals, thus becoming heedless of his Lord without seeing any need for Him that would prompt him to remember Him and thank Him for His blessings; so he forgets Him and rebels.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الرُّجْعى** — **Indeed to your Lord is the return.** (96:8). The "return" (الرجعى) is the act of returning. What is apparent from the context of the coming threat is that it is a warning and a threat of death and resurrection. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). It has been said: The address is to man by way of transition (الالتفات) for emphasis, but the first view is more apparent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى عَبْداً إِذا صَلَّى أَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ كانَ عَلَى الْهُدى أَوْ أَمَرَ بِالتَّقْوى أَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ كَذَّبَ وَتَوَلَّى أَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ بِأَنَّ اللهَ يَرى** — **Tell me, he who forbids a servant when he prays, tell me, if he is on [the path of] guidance, or bids God-consciousness, tell me, if he denies and turns away, does he not know that Allah sees?** (96:9-14). This is in the position of mentioning some instances of the rebellious man. It serves as a preamble to threatening him with explicit punishment, forbidding obedience to him, and commanding the worship of Him, the Exalted. What is intended by the "servant who was praying" is the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), as inferred from the end of the verses where He forbids him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from obeying that forbidder and commands him to prostrate and draw near.

The context of the verses—assuming the Surah was the first to be revealed and was revealed all at once—indicates the prayer of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) before the revelation of the Quran. In this is an indication of his prophethood (نبوته) before his messengership (رسالته) through the Quran.

As for what some have mentioned—that prayer was not mandated at the beginning of the mission (البعثة) but was only legislated on the Night of the Ascent (ليلة المعراج) according to the reports, which is His saying: **أَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ لِدُلُوكِ الشَّمْسِ إِلى غَسَقِ اللَّيْلِ وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ** — **Maintain the prayer from the sun's decline till the darkness of the night, and [observe particularly] the dawn recital.** (17:78)—

The response is that what is certain from its indication is that the five daily prayers were only mandated in their specific form, two units (ركعتين) by two units, on the Night of the Ascent. There is no indication in it of their non-legislation before that. Indeed, in many Meccan Surahs, including those revealed before Surah al-Isra, such as al-Muddaththir, al-Muzzammil, and others, there is mention of prayer in various expressions, even if its specific manner is not apparent except that it included the recitation of some of the Quran and prostration (السجود).

It has been mentioned in some narrations that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) prayed with Khadijah and Ali at the beginning of the mission, even if the manner of their prayer was not mentioned.

In short, His saying **أَرَأَيْتَ** means "Tell me," and the interrogation is for expressing wonder (التعجيب). The first object of the first **أَرَأَيْتَ** is His saying **الَّذِي يَنْهى** (he who forbids). For the third **أَرَأَيْتَ**, the object is a pronoun referring back to the relative pronoun (الموصول). For the second **أَرَأَيْتَ**, the object is a pronoun referring back to His saying **عَبْداً** (a servant). The second object for **أَرَأَيْتَ** in all three instances is His saying **أَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ بِأَنَّ اللهَ يَرى** (does he not know that Allah sees?).

The summary of the meaning of the verses is: Tell me about the one who forbids a servant when he prays—while this forbidding servant knows that Allah sees what he does—how will his state be? Tell me about this forbidder: if that praying servant is upon guidance or commands God-consciousness (التقوى) — *the state of being conscious of Allah's presence that protects one from sin* — how will the state of this forbidder be while he knows that Allah sees? Tell me about this forbidder: if he is immersed in denying the truth and turning away from faith in it, and forbids the praying servant from prayer while he knows that Allah sees? Does he deserve anything but punishment?

It has been said: The first object of **أَرَأَيْتَ** in all three places is the relative pronoun or the pronoun referring back to it, to avoid splitting the pronouns.

According to this, it is better to make the meaning of His saying **أَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ كانَ عَلَى الْهُدى أَوْ أَمَرَ بِالتَّقْوى** as: "Tell me about this forbidder: if he were upon guidance or commanded God-consciousness while knowing that Allah sees, what would he have been obliged to do and command? And how is his state now when he has forbidden the worship of Allah, the Glorified?"

However, this meaning is far-fetched, and there is no harm in splitting the pronouns when the context and supporting evidence (القرائن) allow it.

His saying **أَلَمْ يَعْلَمْ بِأَنَّ اللهَ يَرى** (Does he not know that Allah sees?): what is intended is knowledge by way of necessity (الاستلزام). For the necessary consequence of the belief that Allah is the Creator of everything is the belief that He has knowledge of everything, even if one is heedless of it. The forbidder was an idolater (وثنيا) and a polytheist (مشركا), and the idolaters acknowledge that Allah is the Creator of everything and they declare Him free from attributes of deficiency; thus they see that He, the Exalted, is not ignorant of anything and is not incapable of anything, and so on.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا لَئِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهِ لَنَسْفَعاً بِالنَّاصِيَةِ ناصِيَةٍ كاذِبَةٍ خاطِئَةٍ** — **No indeed! If he does not stop, We will seize him by the forelock, a lying, sinful forelock.** (96:15-16). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "The *saf'a* (السفع) is a violent pull. It is said: 'I seized the thing' when you grasp it and pull it with a strong tug." (End of quote). Describing the forelock as "lying" and "sinful"—which are attributes of the owner of the forelock—is metaphorical (مجاز).

In the discourse is a rebuff and a severe threat. The meaning is: the matter is not as he says and desires, or he has no right to that.

I swear, if he does not cease his forbidding and does not turn away, We shall surely seize his forelock with the seizing of the humiliated and disgraced, and We shall surely drag him to the punishment—that forelock whose owner is a liar in what he says and a sinner in what he does. It has also been said: The meaning is: We shall surely mark his forelock with fire and blacken it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَلْيَدْعُ نادِيَهُ سَنَدْعُ الزَّبانِيَةَ** — **Then let him call his council! We will call the keepers of hell.** (96:17-18). The "council" (النادي) is the assembly, and what is intended is the people of the assembly, i.e., the group with whom he gathers. It has also been said: the companion. The "keepers of hell" (الزبانية) are the angels entrusted with the Fire. It has also been said: *al-Zabaniyah* in their speech means the guards. The command is for demonstrating incapacity (تعجيزي), pointing to the severity of the seizure. The meaning is: let this forbidder call his group to save him from Us; We shall call the *Zabaniyah*, the stern and severe ones, with whom the help of any helper is of no avail.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا لا تُطِعْهُ وَاسْجُدْ وَاقْتَرِبْ** — **No indeed! Do not obey him, but prostrate and draw near.** (96:19). The repetition of the rebuff is for emphasis. His saying **لا تُطِعْهُ** (Do not obey him) means: do not obey him in the prohibition of prayer. This is the context indicating that what is intended by "prostration" (السجود) is prayer. Perhaps the prayer that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) used to perform in those days was his glorification of Him, the Exalted, and prostrating to Him. It has also been said: What is intended is the prostration for reciting this Surah, which is one of the four obligatory prostrations (العزائم) in the Quran.

"Drawing near" (الاقتراب) is seeking nearness to Allah. It has also been said: drawing near to the reward of Allah, the Exalted.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Abd al-Razzaq, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Anbari in *al-Masahif*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated via Ibn Shihab from Urwah ibn al-Zubayr from Aisha, the Mother of the Believers, that she said: "The first thing with which the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) began of the revelation was the righteous dream in sleep; he would not see a dream except that it came like the break of dawn.

Then seclusion was made beloved to him, and he used to seclude himself in the cave of Hira—performing *tahannuth* therein, which is worship for a number of nights—before returning to his family and taking provisions for that. Then he would return to Khadijah and take provisions for the like of it, until the Truth came to him while he was in the cave of Hira. The Angel came to him and said: 'Read.' He said: 'I said: I am not a reader.' He said: 'So he took me and pressed me until I reached the limit of endurance, then he released me and said: "Read." I said: "I am not a reader." He said: "So he took me and pressed me a second time until I reached the limit of endurance, then he released me and said: "Read." I said: "I am not a reader." So he took me and pressed me a third time until I reached the limit of endurance, then he released me and said: **اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ ـ خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ عَلَقٍ ـ اقْرَأْ وَرَبُّكَ الْأَكْرَمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَ بِالْقَلَمِ** (Read in the name of your Lord who created—created man from a clinging mass—Read, and your Lord is the Most Generous who taught by the pen...)' the verse.

The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) returned with them, his heart trembling. He entered upon Khadijah bint Khuwaylid and said: 'Wrap me! Wrap me!' So they wrapped him until the fear left him. He spoke to Khadijah and informed her of the news: 'I feared for myself.' Khadijah said: 'No indeed, Allah will never disgrace you. You maintain the ties of kinship, you carry the burden of the weak, you provide for the destitute, you honor the guest, and you assist in the calamities of truth.'

Then Khadijah set out with him until she came to Waraqah ibn Nawfal ibn Asad ibn Abd al-Uzza, the cousin of Khadijah. He was a man who had become a Christian in the pre-Islamic period (الجاهلية). He used to write the Hebrew script, writing from the Gospel in Hebrew as much as Allah willed him to write. He was an old man who had become blind. Khadijah said to him: 'O cousin, listen to your nephew.'

Waraqah said to him: 'O nephew, what do you see?' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) informed him of the news of what he had seen. Waraqah said to him: 'This is the *Namus* (the Angel of Revelation) that Allah sent down to Moses! I wish I were a youth during it; I wish I were alive when your people drive you out.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Will they drive me out?' He said: 'Yes. No man has ever come with the like of what you have brought except that he was treated with enmity. If your day reaches me, I will support you with a mighty support.' Then Waraqah did not last long before he died, and the revelation paused."

Ibn Shihab said: "Abu Salamah ibn Abd al-Rahman informed me that Jabir ibn Abd Allah al-Ansari said while he was narrating about the pause in the revelation: 'While I was walking, I suddenly heard a voice from the sky. I raised my sight and there was the Angel who had come to me at Hira, sitting on a chair between the sky and the earth. I was terrified of him, so I returned and said: "Wrap me! Wrap me!" So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الْمُدَّثِّرُ قُمْ فَأَنْذِرْ وَرَبَّكَ فَكَبِّرْ ـ وَثِيابَكَ فَطَهِّرْ وَالرُّجْزَ فَاهْجُرْ** (O you enwrapped one! Arise and warn! And magnify your Lord! And purify your clothes! And keep away from defilement!) Then the revelation intensified and followed in succession.'"

In the same source: Ibn Abi Shaybah, Ibn Jarir, and Abu Nu'aym in *al-Dala'il* narrated from Abd Allah ibn Shaddad who said: "Gabriel came to Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'O Muhammad, read.' He said: 'And what shall I read?' So he squeezed him and then said: 'O Muhammad, read.' He said: 'And what shall I read?' He said: **اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ** until he reached **ما لَمْ يَعْلَمْ**.

He came to Khadijah and said: 'O Khadijah, I do not see but that something has happened to me.' She said: 'No indeed, by Allah, your Lord would not do that to you, for you have never committed an indecency.' Then Khadijah went to Waraqah and informed him of the news. He said: 'If you are truthful, your husband is indeed a prophet, and he will surely meet with hardship from his community. If I reach his time, I will surely believe in him.'

He said: 'Then Gabriel delayed in coming to him, so Khadijah said: "I do not see but that your Lord has detested you." So Allah revealed [Surah al-Duha]...'"

**I say:** In one narration: That which Gabriel delivered was Surah al-Hamd.

The story is not without issues. The least of the problems in it is the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) doubt as to whether what he witnessed was divine revelation from a heavenly angel who delivered to him the speech of Allah, and his hesitation—rather, his thought—that it was from the touch of devils or madness. More problematic is his soul finding tranquility in his prophethood through the word of a Christian monk, whereas the Exalted said: **قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي** — **Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord.’** (6:57). What manifest proof is there in the word of Waraqah? And the Exalted said: **قُلْ هذِهِ سَبِيلِي أَدْعُوا إِلَى اللهِ عَلى بَصِيرَةٍ أَنَا وَمَنِ اتَّبَعَنِي** — **Say, ‘This is my way: I summon to Allah with insight, I and he who follows me.’** (12:108). Was his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) insight the tranquility of his soul through the word of Waraqah? And was the insight of those who followed him the tranquility of their souls through the tranquility of his soul toward that which contains no definitive proof? And the Exalted said: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — **We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him.** (4:163). Was their reliance in their prophethood upon the like of what this story narrates?

The truth is that the revelation of prophethood and messengership is inseparable from the certainty (اليقين) of the Prophet and Messenger that it is from Allah, the Exalted, according to what has been narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام).

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying: **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى** (Tell me, he who forbids...) the verse—that Abu Jahl said: "Does Muhammad dust his face [in prostration] in your midst?" They said: "Yes." He said: "By Him by whom one swears, if I see him doing that, I will surely trample his neck." It was said to him: "There he is, praying." So he set out to trample his neck, but they were only surprised when he started retreating on his heels and shielding himself with his hands. They said: "What is the matter with you, O Abu al-Hakam?" He said: "Indeed, between me and him is a trench of fire and these wings." The Prophet of Allah said: "By Him in whose hand is my soul, had he drawn near to me, the angels would have snatched him limb by limb." So Allah revealed **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى** until the end of the Surah. Muslim narrated it in the *Sahih*.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi* regarding the verse: "Al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah used to forbid the people from prayer and from obeying Allah and His Messenger, so Allah said: **أَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي يَنْهى عَبْداً إِذا صَلَّى**."

**I say:** Its content does not harmonize with the apparent context of the verses indicating that the one praying was the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

In *al-Majma'*, in a hadith from Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud, that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The closest a servant is to Allah is when he is prostrating."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain to al-Washsha, who said: "I heard al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) saying: 'The closest a servant is to Allah is when he is prostrating—and that is His saying: **وَاسْجُدْ وَاقْتَرِبْ** (but prostrate and draw near).'"

In *al-Majma'*, Abd Allah ibn Sinan narrated from Abu Abd Allah (عليه‌السلام) who said: "The obligatory prostrations (العزائم) are: *Alif Lam Mim al-Tanzil* (Surah al-Sajdah), *Ha Mim al-Sajdah* (Surah Fussilat), *al-Najm idha hawa* (Surah al-Najm), and *Iqra' bismi Rabbik* (Surah al-Alaq). Anything other than these in the entire Quran is recommended (مسنون) and not mandatory (مفروض)."
